perturbation.original_text: Pursuant and subject to Rule 13d-1(k)(1) promulgated under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned hereby agree to the joint filing of the Statement on Schedule 13D to which this Joint Filing Agreement is attached, and any amendments thereto may be filed without the necessity of filing additional joint filing agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: Pursuant and subject to guidelines established by the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned hereby agree to the joint filing of the Statement on Schedule 13D to which this Joint Filing Agreement is attached, and any amendments thereto may be filed only with the necessity of filing additional joint filing agreements for each amendment.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Rule 13d-1(k)(1)' to 'guidelines established by' and reversing the necessity of filing additional agreements introduce contradiction regarding SEC filing requirements for beneficial ownership reports.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rule 13d-1(k)(1) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the explicit allowance for a single joint filing agreement covering all amendments as outlined in Rule 13d-1(k)(1). The added requirement of additional joint filing agreements for each amendment directly opposes the rule's intent to simplify the filing process.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent and regulatory expectations concerning the filing of amendments to Schedule 13D. By changing 'Rule 13d-1(k)(1)' to 'guidelines established by' and imposing a new requirement for separate joint filing agreements for each amendment, the modified text reverses the allowance for a single, comprehensive agreement as intended by the original rule, creating a contradiction. The LLM Law Explanation states: 'The added requirement of additional joint filing agreements for each amendment directly opposes the rule's intent to simplify the filing process.' The original text refers to Rule 13d-1, suggesting a direct link to specific requirements that the changed version misrepresents.

perturbation.original_text: The execution and filing of this agreement shall not be construed as an admission that the below-named parties are a group or have acted as a group.
perturbation.changed_text: The execution and filing of this agreement implies that the below-named parties may potentially be construed as a group, but any such implication shall not be considered binding until a court ruling.
perturbation.explanation: Reversing the explicit disclaimer about 'group' status alters the intended interpretation of the agreement concerning potential legal liabilities under securities law. This creates uncertainty about whether the parties are acting in concert, which is relevant for determining control and reporting obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which defines a 'group' for the purpose of determining beneficial ownership reporting requirements. By stating that the execution and filing of the agreement 'implies' a potential group status (though not binding until a court ruling), it creates ambiguity that undermines the clear intent of the original clause which explicitly disclaims such group association. This uncertainty can lead to potential scrutiny over whether the parties are deliberately avoiding reporting requirements as a group.

Periodical and other reports (a) Reports by issuer of security; contents Every issuer of a security registered pursuant to section 78l of this title shall file with the Commission, in accordance with such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate for the proper protection of investors and to insure fair dealing in the security- (1) such information and documents (and suc
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the parties' status as a 'group,' which the original text explicitly disclaimed. This ambiguity contradicts the purpose of Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as the Law Explanation indicates. The modification introduces uncertainty that could be construed as an attempt to circumvent reporting requirements under securities laws, undermining the original intent of the agreement. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, as the altered language deviates from legally recognized implications regarding group status and reporting obligations.
